food_subgroup: Noodle dishes — wheat noodle soup with tofu and seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class (FOODON_00002802). No specific Inari Udon entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers cooked udon noodles as the dominant component. No composite Inari Udon dish code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used for the primary starch component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Inari Udon or udon noodle dish at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Inari Udon as served at a Japanese casual restaurant in Singapore, approximately 400 g, comprising udon noodles in dashi broth topped with one or two inari age pouches and crabstick.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodle dish, composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Udon noodles cooked (FDC ID 168917), Inari age / seasoned fried tofu pouch (FDC ID 172443), Imitation crab / surimi (FDC ID 175168), and dashi broth components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Inari Udon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: approximately 55% cooked udon noodles, 20% inari age (seasoned fried tofu pouch), 10% surimi/crabstick, and 15% dashi broth with seasoning. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for each component. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a restaurant-style bowl.

gi_source.title: Udon noodles, white, boiled — GI value 55 (reference glucose = 100)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Inari Udon as a composite dish. The GI of 55 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain boiled white udon noodles, which is the dominant glycemic component (~55% by weight). The inari age and crabstick components contribute minimal additional glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (55 × 15.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 400 g serving) / 100 = approximately 34.8 g. GI of 55 falls within the low GI category (55 or less is low; 56–69 is medium; 70+ is high).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth, soy sauce, and seasoned inari age
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g bowl of Inari Udon may deliver approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 55) from refined wheat udon noodles combined with moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Inari Udon has a low GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~34.8 per standard bowl, making it a moderate glycemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control to half a bowl and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables is recommended to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from fried tofu pouch; low saturated fat overall
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While saturated fat content is relatively low at approximately 0.4 g per 100 g, the high sodium load from the seasoned broth poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for reduced-sodium versions and limit consumption frequency to occasional meals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Inari Udon (いなりうどん) is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles served in a warm, savoury dashi broth, topped with inari age — sweet-and-savoury seasoned deep-fried tofu pouches — and crabstick (surimi). The udon noodles are made from wheat flour and water, boiled until soft and slightly springy in texture. The inari age pouches are made from aburaage (thin fried tofu) simmered in a mixture of soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and dashi, giving them a sweet, umami-rich flavour that contrasts pleasantly with the mild, savoury broth. Crabstick (imitation crab made from surimi fish paste) adds a mild seafood note and additional protein. The dish is a popular comfort food in Japan and is commonly found in Japanese casual dining restaurants and convenience stores. In Singapore, it is served at Japanese chain restaurants such as Sushi Express. Nutritionally, Inari Udon is a moderate-energy dish (~98 kcal per 100 g) with a carbohydrate-dominant macronutrient profile from the udon noodles, moderate protein from tofu and surimi, and low saturated fat. However, sodium content is notably high due to the seasoned broth and inari age, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (~55) requires portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains wheat gluten, soy, and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Inari Udon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked udon noodles, seasoned fried tofu pouch (inari age), surimi/crabstick, and dashi broth. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain boiled udon noodles as the dominant glycemic component. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct HPB Singapore or USDA composite entry for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries; no single approved-source record for Inari Udon as a complete dish was available at time of processing. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1.0 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate; the value is considered missing data and cannot be reliably derived from the component-level records available. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in all component entries per USDA FoodData Central. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white udon noodles; no direct GI measurement for Inari Udon as a composite dish was found. 5. Halal status is marked false as inari age may contain mirin (rice wine) and dashi may contain bonito (fish); halal certification would depend on specific restaurant preparation. 6. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects a moderate-sodium restaurant preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on broth concentration and portion of inari age used. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002802 and FoodEx2 code A0EJW are used as closest_parent matches; no exact composite dish codes exist for Inari Udon in either ontology at time of processing.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for udon noodles, inari age, surimi, and dashi broth.
Iteration 1: Added Japanese local name いなりうどん to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Japanese cuisine.
Iteration 1: Set GI to 55 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for boiled white udon noodles; documented estimation basis in gi_source.
Iteration 1: Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1.0 sentinel value in notes as required by G3 sentinel documentation rule.
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all Japanese and English alias strings appear verbatim in both structures.

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, aligning with the definition of low GI (55 or less). Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor/guidance accordingly.

Iteration 3: Strengthened G3 sentinel documentation for vitamin_d_mcg = -1.0 in notes field to explicitly state the value is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate, satisfying the missing-data basis documentation requirement that caused the previous REJECT decision.
